user_query:
I’m setting up a lightweight weekly scan to support my research on diplomacy in conflict prevention, and I need this to run automatically and produce actionable notes for our lab’s Monday check-ins. Please implement a small, cross‑platform scheduled job inside this workspace with a one‑time dry‑run I can trigger now. Use the provided inputs at input/watchlist.yaml and input/meeting_template.md.

Requirements
- Scheduling and execution
  - Create config/schedule.json that schedules the job for every Monday at 08:30 local time.
  - Implement scripts/scheduler.py that reads config/schedule.json and uses an in‑process scheduler (e.g., a library or time loop) to run the job at the specified time when the scheduler is started.
  - Also provide an immediate dry‑run command to execute the job once for testing (e.g., a script scripts/run_once.[sh|py] or a documented command) that pipes both stdout and stderr to logs/dry_run.log.
- Web search and data capture
  - For each combination of topics and source_selectors in input/watchlist.yaml, perform a search using any public web search engine of your choice (record which engine you used in metadata). Use the source selector’s query_modifier (e.g., domain patterns like un.org, osce.org, au.int) together with the topic string to form each search query. Do not require credentials or paywalled access; use the public search engine’s general web results.
  - For each query, capture the first page of results and save:
    1) Raw SERP HTML to data/YYYY-MM-DD/serp_<source_label>_<topic_slug>.html (one file per query), and
    2) A structured line‑delimited JSON file at data/YYYY-MM-DD/results.jsonl with one object per result containing: timestamp, source_label, topic, query, search_engine, rank, title, url, and snippet. Deduplicate by URL and keep up to the top 10 unique results per query.
- Logging and diagnostics
  - All job runs (scheduled or dry‑run) must write both stdout and stderr to a timestamped log at logs/YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS_run.log. The dry‑run must additionally write to logs/dry_run.log.
  - After the dry‑run completes, parse logs/dry_run.log to identify and count errors and warnings (look for lines that include common markers like ERROR, Exception, WARN, or non‑zero exit traces). This analysis must be reflected in the meeting notes (see below).
- Meeting notes and action items
  - Generate meeting_notes/weekly_brief.md by filling in the sections from input/meeting_template.md using the dry‑run outputs. Include:
    • Key Findings: up to five items aggregated across sources with source_label, title, and URL.
    • Action Items: at least one concrete next step per source_label that yielded results (e.g., “Review OSCE brief X and summarize for seminar” with a due date). If a source had no results, note “No new items this cycle” for that source.
    • Diagnostics: number of queries attempted, number of SERPs saved, number of result entries written, count of errors and warnings from logs/dry_run.log, the first one or two representative error messages if any, the search engine used, and the path to the primary run log.
    • Appendix: list the exact topic and query_modifier pairs used, pulled from input/watchlist.yaml, so we can cross‑check the query matrix.

Acceptance criteria (artifacts must exist in the workspace)
- config/schedule.json with Monday 08:30 local time.
- scripts/scheduler.py and a documented way to start it for ongoing weekly execution (it may run in the foreground; system‑level schedulers are optional).
- An immediate dry‑run command or script that executes the job once and writes to logs/dry_run.log.
- data/YYYY-MM-DD/ with at least one serp_*.html per query and a results.jsonl containing the required fields.
- logs/YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS_run.log for the dry‑run (plus logs/dry_run.log capturing the same run’s stdout/stderr).
- meeting_notes/weekly_brief.md filled from input/meeting_template.md, including the Diagnostics section that reflects analysis of the dry‑run’s logs.

Use only the domain patterns and topics from input/watchlist.yaml to build queries; do not hardcode direct URLs. Please keep the implementation minimal but robust enough to handle transient HTTP or parsing issues and to surface any such issues in the Diagnostics section. All file paths are relative to the workspace directory.
